title_tesim: Joey Walsh in Hans Christian Andersen
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Joey Walsh is, in reality, going to correspondence school while making "Hans Christian Andersen." His lesson outlines and study contents are mailed out each week from New York by the Children's Professional School, a famous school for theatrical moppets (the one that Mildred Dunnock headed.) Samuel Goldwyn hired Jean Seaman here to handle this end of it. He's winding up the eighth grade now and in June will be graduated from junior High School. Joey insisted that he not sever his relations with the Children's Professional School because he wants to stay in the class with his schoolmates, ones he's been with since the first grade. Among them are Iris Mann, the little girl that was so good in "Room For One More" with Cary Grant, and Clifford Sales of the "Date With Judy Show." SG-6800-P-56/ SG-6800-P-55
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen, Cesar Romero, and Bobby Howes in Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Theatrical producer John Frost (Cesar Romero) is dazed and speechless. He has just been presented with a cheque for ten thousand pounds, but Janet Jones (Vera-Ellen), leading lady in his show, tells him that the man who gave him the cheque is not B.G. Bruno the millionaire, but an imposter. Charlie the stage manager (Bobby Howes) looks up sympathetically at his boss. A scene from Happy-Go-Lovely, the Associated British-Marcel Hellman Technicolor musical set against the background of the Edinburgh Festival. David Niven, Vara-Ellen and Cesar Romero are the stars. Bruce Humberstone directs.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Director Bruce Humberstone and other production crew members on the set of Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: This earnest-looking group on the set of Happy-Go-Lovely at Elstree studios consists of Director Bruce Humberstone, Script Adviser Desmond Davis and Continuity Girl Constance Newton. Together they are working out the lines to be spoken by Vera-Ellen, Cesar Romero and David Niven, who co-star in this Associated British-Marcel Hellman production in Technicolor.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen and Cesar Romero in Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: A charming portrait of Hollywood stars Vera-Ellen and Cesar Romero who appear together in the new Associated British-Marcel Hellman technicolor production Happy-Go-Lovely directed by Bruce Humberstone. Vera-Ellen takes the role of Janet Jones, chorus girl at the Mercury Theatre, Edinburgh, who becomes the star of the show. Cesar Romero plays John Frost, fast-talking, wisecracking producer who is constantly finding himself in financial difficulties. David Niven also stars in Happy-Go-Lovely.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Van Johnson and Gene Kelly in Brigadoon
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Look Who's Dancing!...It's Van Johnson, left, dancing alongside Gene Kelly for the first time in thirteen years as the two pair up in a comedy hoofing number of M-G-M's "Brigadoon". The last time they combined musical talents was in the 1941 hit, "Pal Joey" -- but there was one difference. Then, Johnson was just an unheralded dancer in the chorus and Kelly was the stage's newest star. Now, in the new Cinema-Scope musical, they're co-stars, along with Cyd Charisse, and Johnson is dancing for only the second time in pictures for the nostalgic reunion. Vincente Minnelli directs the new film, produced by Arthur Freed.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Claudette Colbert in Zaza
caption_tesim: Claudette Colbert in "Zaza," with Herbert Marshall, Bert Lahr, Helen Westley, Constance Collier, Genevieve Tobin and Walter Catlett. Directed by George Cukor. A Paramount Picture.

Text from back of photograph: PIQUANT– in the most piquant role she has yet attempted, Claudette Colbert wears the frills of the early 1900's, sings and dances the can-can in Paramount's "Zaza," in which she is now starring with Herbert Marshall opposite her. A fascinating music hall entertainer, she wins Marshall away from his wife in the picture, only to give him back. The picture is antique in setting but has a modern day spice and the picturesque charm and vivacity of the theater in its heyday.

Starring ROCK HUDSON, BARBARA HALE, ANTHONY QUINN and RICHARD CARLSON, the screenplay is by Charles K. Peck, Jr., direction by Budd Boetticher, and production by Howard Christie for Universal-International. The story in pictures:3. Lance is ordered on patrol, with a sergeant assigned by Major Degan, the commanding officer, to spy on him because of his Seminole sympathies - and Revere slips away into the Everglades to visit the village of Osceola. title_tesim: Barbara Stanwyck being hooked up to an electro-cardiograph apparatus in Sorry, Wrong Number
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Barbara Stanwyck gave the most realistic performance of her long Hollywood career during the filming of a hospital scene in Hal Wallis' "Sorry, Wrong Number." In the suspense movie based on the famous radio thriller she plays the neurotic wife (of Burt Lancaster) who over hears her own murder being plotted on the telephone. For the scene in which her heart is tested to determine if anything is organically wrong, director Anatole Litvak used a real electro-cardiograph machine and trained operators. When the camera stopped turning an actual graph of the star's heart beats had been recorded in red ink on the paper chart. Here is what happened when the true-to-fact scene was filmed. 1. A technician takes a pulse count of Barbara Stanwyck before the electro-cardiograph apparatus is attached. Miss S. passes O.K. and says "Let's go, boys." [Stamped] Photo By, Mal. Bulloch
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: Although they appear in the film, "Guerrilla Girl," there are no credits listed for these two unidentified actors.

Excerpted from the AFI listing:"Guerrilla Girl" marked Greek producer-director John Christian's first American feature release. According to a Los Angeles Times news item, the actress billed as Marianna was working as Christian's production secretary before being cast in the film. A modern source lists the working title as "Dawn Comes Late."

Greek film producer/director, Giannis Hristodoulou, was credited in the film, "Guerrilla Girl" as, John Christian. He has also been credited in other films as, Jan Christian and Ioannis Hristodoulou, as well as by his birth name, Giannis Hristodoulou.
AFI lists a release date and copyright date for the film, "Guerrilla Girl,' as January 23, 1953.

title_tesim: Jesse White and Victoria Horne in a scene from Harvey
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: Universal-International, Universal City, Calif."Harvey"--- A Pictorial Synopsis"Harvey" is the movie presentation of Mary Chase's Pulitzer Prize play of 1945 - the award "which was won by a hare." The play about an invisible rabbit who, oddly enough, was viewed by theatregoers on Broadway for more than four years, is the most expensive dramatic property ever to reach the screen, topping "Life with Father" in its cost of a million dollars plus profit percentage for movie rights. It is presented with the same spirit and characterization which made it unforgettable theatre. Starring JAMES STEWART who twice replaced Frank Fay in the original company, featuring JOSEPHINE HULL, pixie personality of the stage (familiar in movies from "Arsenic and Old Lace") and introducing JESSE WHITE of the stage company, the cast will command consideration at Academy Award time. Directed by Henry Koster, "Harvey" was produced by John Beck for Universal-International. The story in pictures:6. Wilson goes to the Dowd residence, doesn't find Elwood, but discovers lonesone Myrtle Mae - and her romantic life gets a big lift. Wilson goes for her and her onion sandwiches! Veta escapes Chumley's Rest in the confusion, calls her lawyer, Judge Gaffney, and orders him to sue Dr. Chumley for holding her on what she considers must be at least a white slavery charge.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen and Richard Todd in London after filming Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: During the filming of Happy-Go-Lovely at Elstree studios, Hollywood dancing star Vera-Ellen had no chance of looking round London. She was either rehearsing or before the cameras every day of the production. So when at last, after five months hard work, her part in the film was completed Vera-Ellen decided to see the famous landmarks of England's capital for herself. Her guide and companion for the day was Britain's top male star, Richard (Hasty Heart) Todd, who was himself sightseeing in Hollywood only a few months earlier. Together, the two made a lightning tour of the city, visiting such well-known spots as Piccadilly Circus, Trafalgar Square, Fleet Street, Green Park and the Houses of Parliament. Seated on the Embankment, beside the River Thames, Richard Todd and Vera-Ellen consult a map on London to decide what they ought to see next.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Unidenitified dancers in Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: First of the four big musical production numbers in Happy-Go-Lovely is Mackintosh's Wedding, which tells - in terms of music, song and dancing - the story of a Highland wedding. The bride (Joan Heal) keeps up a running chase before finally leading the man of her dreams to the altar steps. The number takes place on the stage of the Mercury Theatre, Edinburgh, where Vera-Ellen, Diane Hart and Sandra Dorne are chorus girls in a show produced by John Frost (Cesar Romero).The original music for Mackintosh's Wedding was written by Mischa Spoliansky and the dances were arranged by Pauline Grant.Happy-Go-Lovely is an Associated British-Marcel Hellman production in Technicolor starring David Niven, Vera-Ellen and Cesar Romero. Bruce Humberstone directs.
